results.possibleIdentity: Jason Roger Crum (1935–2004) was an American abstract painter and a pioneering muralist active in New York City during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known as a co-founder of City Walls, Inc. (established in 1967), a non-profit organization that transformed the urban landscape by commissioning artists to paint large-scale geometric abstract murals on blank building facades. His work is often characterized as "supergraphics," featuring bold primary colors and hard-edge geometric shapes designed to revitalize neighborhoods. One of his earliest and most notable projects was a 1967 mural on East 9th Street in Manhattan. Crum's influence extended to major institutions; his work is held in the permanent collection of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), which featured his project "Project for a Painted Wall, New York City" in the 1969 exhibition "Painting for City Walls." He was part of a circle of artists who sought to move art out of galleries and into public spaces, collaborating with figures such as Allan D'Arcangelo and Tania. Historically, Crum is associated with the intersection of art, urban planning, and technology-driven environments of the era. He participated in events and publications that featured Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and its members, such as a 1970 event involving a large pneumatic "bubble" structure designed by Yukihisa Isobe (an E.A.T. collaborator). Crum eventually moved to Colorado, where he continued his artistic practice until his death in 2004.

results.possibleIdentity: Ronald Mallory (1932–2021) was an American kinetic artist and architect who was a prominent figure in the New York art scene during the 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his innovative 'mercury pictures' and kinetic sculptures, which used liquid mercury encased in motorized, rotating acrylic containers to create ever-shifting organic forms. Born in Philadelphia, Mallory initially studied architecture at the University of Florida and worked in Brazil under the renowned modernist Oscar Niemeyer and landscape architect Roberto Burle Marx, the latter of whom encouraged him to focus on fine art. After living in Paris and exhibiting at the Galerie Iris Clert, he moved to New York City, where he shared a studio with kinetic artist Pol Bury. Mallory was closely associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, a collaboration between artists and engineers; his name is included in the E.A.T. sub-series of the Robert Rauschenberg papers. His work often involved technical experimentation, such as his collaboration with the Monsanto chemical company to develop specialized fluids for his sculptures. Mallory had a major solo exhibition at the Stable Gallery in 1966 and his works are held in the permanent collections of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art. He was married to the actress and author Carole Mallory from 1968 to 1971.
